speaker: interviewer
text: March the 18th, 1996. Survivor, Rabbi Isaac Richards. Interviewer, Padigail Meskin, in the city of Durban. Country, South Africa. Language, English. My name is Padigail Meskin. I shall be interviewing survivor Rabbi Isaac Richards in the city of Durban, South Africa, on March the 18th, 1996. Rabbi, please, could you tell us your name and your birth date and your birth place, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Isaac Richards. My birth date is the 23rd of September, 1913, and I was born in Liverpool. May I just correct one thing? I'm not actually a survivor. I was not in any of the concentration camps, except in the capacity of a chaplain to the British forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. We-- we consider all people who were in contact with them as survivors. Thank you, rabbi. Could you tell us a little bit about life in the community that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liverpool is a very large city, a port. At the time, it was one of the major ports of England. And Jewishly, there were some 7,000 Jewish people who lived in Liverpool. There must have been quite a lot of synagogue goers, because there were at least five big synagogues, apart from half a dozen smaller what we call [HEBREW], so that people were very well catered for, religiously. And there was, of course, a-- a Jewish education, which was usually after school. There were no Jewish day schools. The Jewish education was given after the ordinary day school, and attended what was called a cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the organizations and institutions? Were those--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the normal organizations operated, from the [HEBREW], the [HEBREW], to the helping a poor bride and caring for the poor and the sick, and so on. All the usual institutions of a Jewish community were there in full sway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your own personal religious background, what kind of a background did you come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came from what would be called, by modern standards, a pretty orthodox, and certainly observant. [INAUDIBLE] and such like things were strictly observed in our home, and I think a great majority of the people at that time were-- the older people, certainly, followed that path of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a very large family, by today's standards. I am the seventh child out of eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was quite a-- quite a sit down, when you sat down to a Seder. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: At all times, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi, can you tell me, did you-- do you remember experiencing anti-Semitism, pre-Nazi times?

speaker: interviewee
text: We indeed, did experience rather rough types of anti-Semitism. I call it rough because what used to happen was, that coming and going at your cheder, which was, from my home-- we didn't live in a very Jewish area-- it was about a mile and a half, two miles. And on the way back, we would pass through an area which was a little bit rough. And very often, we were chased by other youngsters, and unpleasant names called after us. And usually one of our older brothers would accompany us, in case we were attacked, or anything like that. There was a lot of that kind of thing amongst the-- the youngsters, who very likely learned it from their parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your actual education, did it all take place in Liverpool?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to a high school. A Jewish school or a government school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no Jewish schools. Jewish school was the cheder, which we attended every afternoon for some three hours. And-- and then I went on to the yeshiva, which was under the leadership of Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], who later became the chief rabbi of Israel. And-- and I continued my studies there at the yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you became a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was qualified by the-- the rabbi-- the Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], the chief rabbi, and served as a-- with the title of Reverend at first, in a little place in North Wales called Bangor, a very pretty place, very pretty indeed, and then in a small industrial town-- well, not so small by South African standards-- called Bolton, near Manchester.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, Rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from about 1937, and first in-- in Bangor for a couple of years, and then in Bolton, until I went-- went into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the rise of Naziism in Germany affect life in-- in England? And in particular, the life of Jews in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, frankly, I don't think it affected very much. Despite what I said about the youngsters persecuting us, we found very little anti-Semitism. Now, my parents, as I said, lived in a completely non-Jewish area. There weren't many Jewish families. And I went to a school where, at one time, I was the only Jew in the school. And every consideration was given. And particularly, I remember, as Passover approaches, that we always used to take some homemade wine and some matzah and give it to the teachers. And the principal would always come and personally visit to thank us, and get an extra bottle of wine.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] So effectively, there was no real--

speaker: interviewee
text: Effectively, there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Increase?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite the-- quite the reverse. We found in our community, that although my mother could hardly speak English, yet she seemed to get on very well with the neighbors. And even the church people were very kind to us and used to throw business my father's way. Because he wasn't a great businessman, and with a big family like that, you can well imagine he had quite a struggle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business did-- was he in, Rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in manufacturing furniture. But he wasn't a practical man himself, so that was a drawback.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you enter the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I volunteered as a chaplain, because ministers were excluded from being called up, and-- and I was called up in 1943, early in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you volunteered at--

speaker: interviewee
text: I volunteered a few years earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the beginning of the war, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Shortly after the beginning of the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what rank did you achieve after basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- well, immediately, one received the rank of captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 1939, when the war started, September '39, did you find an increase in anti-Semitism then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- we didn't experience very much in Bolton, where I lived. But from the reports, from bigger cities, where people spent nights and nights in the shelters, no. I can't recall any major sort of breakouts of anti-Semitism, or anything like that. Everybody was in trouble together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. Mm. What-- what kind of information was filtering through, particularly about the Jews? Did-- did you know about what was happening with the Nuremberg laws, and that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew of the Nuremberg laws. These were reported to us mainly in the Jewish press, the We knew about that, and we knew about Kristallnacht, but we certainly did not know-- did not understand, and hardly could comprehend-- what was going on in the concentration camps and how Jews were being slaughtered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I don't think many people knew, outside, maybe, some of the leadership in London. But many people did not really understand this until the news reels began, on-- particularly the news reels about Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so the establishment of Dachau in 1935, and those--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I doubt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sort of things--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were not known, generally.

speaker: interviewer
text: --didn't even impinge?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get back to your army training and-- and-- and involvement, you went straight into the chaplaincy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went straight into the chaplaincy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a particular unit or corps or division, or were you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the general--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called a chaplaincy corps, and I was stationed at the-- at a place called Colchester, which was the largest intake center for new recruits being brought in from the whole of the London-- of the southern England area. And at any one time, there were many, many thousands of boys coming there for the-- what you call square bashing, after which they were posted to various units.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the basic training camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the basic training camp, and I established a little synagogue on the camp, and we had very-- very-- a very nice arrangement where the boys were free, particularly on Friday night-- sometimes on Saturdays-- and we used to get good gatherings from the recruits who were only too glad to be able to see something of Jewishness, like they were experiencing in their own home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there quite a large Jewish intake into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fair. Oh, yes. Well, everybody had to go, so there was quite a large intake, per capita, of Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there was quite a big Jewish community in England at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and particularly in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you remember who were your commanding officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the faintest idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you please tell us when and how you actually became involved in the-- going to Germany and to the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, as-- as I said, I was stationed in Colchester. And shortly after the Bergen-Belsen camp had been liberated, there was a dire need for at least one or two men, who had some authority, who could represent the displaced persons, as they were then called, who could care for them, could act as liaison for them, with the British forces. And so, I and another Jewish chaplain were posted immediately-- we had about a week's notice-- were posted to Bergen-Belsen direct. And we took off from London.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, Rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 8th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: VE Day.

speaker: interviewee
text: VE day, and you can imagine, Piccadilly circus.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a circus.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a real circus. And we had to report to a little office there, and we got on the bus and were taken to the airport and flew out from England. And we landed in Belgium. We couldn't get transport on for a day or two, so we spent the evening in Brussels. And a day or two later, we landed at an airport near to Bergen-Belsen. We were met by the senior Jewish chaplain, known as Isaac Levy, or Ike Levy, and he took us over to Bergen-Belsen, and, as it were, threw us head-first into what was obviously a tremendously strange experience to us. And, of course, we witnessed the horror of all that was going on. Those who survived were-- had barely survived, and for weeks, many hundreds of people were still dying from the effects of starvation, and even some from the effects of eating the wrong food when the [? Tommies, ?] out of the goodness of their heart, gave them tins of bully beef, and food like that, which the-- what is the word-- their stomachs just couldn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rejected it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --couldn't-- couldn't take-- take in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Just before we go on to Bergen-Belsen, just to go back to your actually being sent to Bergen-- Bergen-Belsen, the decision to send you. At that stage, did you have some idea of what had happened? Had you seen some of the film footage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The films, yes. Seen the-- Oh, yes we'd seen 'em.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had some-- did you have some kind of idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Idea of what-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what you were going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what we were going to-- but we received no instructions from the senior rabbi, who'd never been there, sort of thing. We received no instructions, and we had to work out ourselves, what we were-- had to do, what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you some of the first Jewish chaplains to actually go in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were the first to come and to-- I was the first to come and to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- the-- the chaplain who was with the unit that liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the 15th of April?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 15th-- no. 15th of April, yes. On the 15th of April. He stayed there a few days, and then, as his unit moved on, he moved on with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was attached to a particular unit, where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- yes. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- one would say he was sort of living with a particular unit, you see, as we later did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had to move on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so, you-- you had some kind of idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had an idea of what-- what to see, what-- what we would meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: There had been a lot of publicity in the press?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But how we were to handle it, that was something different. And we had to work that out for ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You arrived in Bergen-Belsen--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- about the 10th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the 10th of May. Can you try and describe to us your first feelings as you saw the camp, and when you first walked in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, it was still a tremendous shock to see mounds of bodies lying there, to see walking skeletons in striped uniforms that they still were wearing. And-- and for them-- if I may digress-- for them, it was a strange and wonderful thing to see a-- an army person with the Magen David on his lapels and on his hat. And as I was not bearded, for a time, they-- they couldn't realize that I was a rabbi, because, to them, a rabbi was somebody with a long-- very long beard, and so on. But they soon got used to us, and-- and were very grateful for everything that we were able to do for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to the original Bergen-Belsen camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The original Bergen-Belsen camp, as it was, as you saw it on the-- on the films.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you had seen on the film footage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a very large camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a tremendously large camp, and it wasn't only for Jews. There were lots and lots of-- of non-Jews in the camp, and Jews from various parts of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many different parts, Rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many different parts. But by the time we got there, many French Jews or Belgian Jews or Holland Jews had begun to be repatriated. What was left was, in the main, Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mainly Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they didn't wish to return, or they couldn't return?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the authorities would have been delighted and wanted to try and return them. You must go back. You came from Poland. You must go back to Poland. You came from Lithuania. You must go back to Lithuania. But the Jews in the camp said, no. We are not going back to places which are merely burial places, which are merely cemeteries. We don't-- Warsaw is no longer a Jewish place. It's just a Jewish cemetery. And the same with all the small towns of Lithuania and Poland. And so, the family determined themselves, and there arose their leaders-- we must mention that a bit later-- and the whole policy was to keep them there until such time-- and in this way, to force the hand-- hands of the British authorities to allow them to go to what then was called Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: This, in fact, was what these-- these people wanted, the majority--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what, in the main--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of these people wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: --these people wanted. And this was the official policy of the Zionist movement, to keep them together. And this leads on a little later to something else I want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that they considered the-- their own hometowns, the places that they had come from, to be burial places. Was that because they already knew that most of the people had been destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew. They knew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there aware of what had happened throughout?

speaker: interviewee
text: And how-- and how they were aware. They were completely aware of what had happened. They were part of it. They were driven out of their hometowns, and they were herded into ghettos. They were herded into trains, taken to Auschwitz and so on, and then onto Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen became overcrowded, because the Russians were advancing so quickly, and the-- and the Germans didn't want the Russians to see what they had done. And so they pushed thousands and thousands from those camps which were nearer to where the Russians were advancing, and marched them for days and days into Bergen-Belsen. And this is why it was bursting at the seams.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is also why there were so many Polish Jews there, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The majority of Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and places like that had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sent on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- what was your reaction to this-- I know it's horror, but can you try and explain a little more how you felt about seeing this, and what was the-- what was your view of the task ahead of you, when you saw all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the task ahead of us, I saw simply is to help them to return to normalcy, to live a reasonably normal life. And this began to be realized in some very small measure. First of all, I must say, pay tribute to some of the British authorities, who did outstanding work. There was a brigadier, I can't remember his name. I know later he was a great rugby man, because he's been to South Africa a number of times. He was a medical man, and he established a hospital for those who were in need of treatment. Now, what happened then was that the old camp, which was ridden with all sort of infectious diseases--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lice, typhoid--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time you went in or out, you had to be sprayed with-- with-- I don't know what it was. White powder to kill the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Some kind of disinfectant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfectant. And-- and they decided to burn down the old camp. And first, they transferred all the survivors to a nearby Panzer barracks, very, very modern, and with a very nice facility. Well, it was for them. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: After what they'd had.

speaker: interviewee
text: At least they had a clean room, and they had-- they had certain toilet facilities. Not in the rooms, of course, but-- but at least it was a tremendous advance on the horror camp in which they had been living, where one on top of the other were-- were defecating and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no facilities, no washing facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hardly-- hardly any facilities, and the sick were lying there dying, and everything was mixed up. And so we transferred these people slowly. And a very interesting facet was that the great majority were women. Somehow or other, they had been able to withstand more than the men. And the majority were women, and many of these-- most of them-- had formed little groups with older women and younger women, and even young-- youngsters, teenagers, and-- and formed little families. And when we were trying to move them, we had to try and move the little families together. And if you had a room which was allocated for five, and the family was six, then you had a bit of a problem. It was at this stage-- if I may interpolate-- it was at this stage that I met a man who became a great figure in the world of the displaced persons, named [? Yossele ?] [? Rosensaft. ?] [? Yossele ?] was a little man, Polish Jew, a former, obviously, union organizer. And he began to help in the organization, not through us, but on his own. And-- and while I was trying to fit-- I'll never forget-- while I was trying to fit some people into a particular room, he came along and tried to undo what I was doing, and I got hold of him by the scruff of his neck, and told him to get away. And mind you, he never held it against me, because we became quite friendly afterwards. And of course, I must mention this, that I wasn't alone. Where they came from exactly, I received the help of a young Jewish woman who was-- had been with a Quaker team. And she left her team, which had gone deeper into Germany, and she offered her services to me. And a Polish-French rabbi who had served with the French-- Polish Army in France and had been a prisoner of war, and he came. And we formed a kind of a threesome, and we worked together and-- looking after these people. And so, they were transferred and settled down there. And [? Yossele ?] [? Rosensaft ?] and the other leaders began to emerge. They started a school. Even later on, after I'd left, I think, it came to fruition. They started a theatre. They had adult classes, and not just for-- for religious. They-- they-- they survivors were not necessarily religious. In fact, I think most of them were not terribly religious. There were the religious groups, but in the main, they were left wing and not particularly interested in religious matters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most-- you said earlier, most of them wanted to go to Palestine-- because they were Zionists or because they just didn't want to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think-- I think because the idea of-- of the land of Israel, now, was the answer to them, after what they had suffered, and were afraid they might suffer in any other country. They wanted to have a homeland of their own. I'm sure that was the motivation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You said that they-- the women were-- were in the majority. What-- were they youngish women, mainly? What-- what-- what do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't think there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it? Because they-- why-- why why did they survive over the men, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think women-- women have got more guts, in some respect, than men. And-- and they have more feeling-- I don't know-- for the future, or what. I-- really, I-- I'm not into being able to answer that question. But as a fact, I think the statistics will show that more women survived. I don't know that there were many very old women, but more in the 50s downwards. And not too many very young children, mostly teenagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because most of them were sent to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were sent away, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they left the camp, when they left the original Bergen-Belsen, were they worried about going somewhere else, or were they eager to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. There, we had no trouble. They were eager to go. They saw the place, and they-- they were eager to go. It wasn't-- it-- it was only a mile, a mile and a quarter away. I don't remember how we got them there. I-- many of them might have walked, even. And so there was little trouble there, over that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you arrived in Bergen-Belsen did this removal take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been about three or four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the original camp for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about four weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you'd had the removal, they-- what happened to the-- to the actual, the original Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, it was burnt down. It was quite amusing, actually. It was ceremonially-- all the brigadiers and the-- and all the big shots came, and they brought flame throwers and-- to burn it down. But the first two shots missed. [LAUGHS] Eventually, they managed to get it ignited, and it was burned to the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nothing was left of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
